persona.name: Hannah Sørensen
persona.education: M.Sc. Computer Science, Technical University of Denmark.
long_term_goals: Operate a release process where every change can be reasoned about end to end
Shrink the codebase her team owns to something one engineer can hold in their head
Spend less of her team's time on manual reconciliation review and more on the parts that need judgment

occupation.title: Senior Backend Engineer
occupation.organization: Norden Settlement (instant retail settlement service jointly owned by Nordic banks)
occupation.description: Owns the reconciliation engine and the settlement-state machine that move funds between member banks during the day.

persona.communication_style: Reads the diff before the PR description. Asks 'what's the rollback?' in design reviews. Quiet in demos, talkative in postmortems.

big_five_traits.openness: Moderate — open to new tooling, but only after she has run it end to end on her laptop

likes: Tools she can run end to end on a laptop

beliefs: A change is not landed until a third engineer can rebuild it from source

projects.title: Reconciliation Engine Migration
projects.description: Multi-quarter migration of the reconciliation engine from a monolith to an event-sourced state machine.
key_contributions: Wrote the replay tooling used to re-derive end-of-day state from the event log
Authored the team's runbook for partial-day reconciliation incidents

general: Keeps a running log of every dependency upgrade landed in production
Refuses to approve a pull request she has not built and run locally

morning: Scans overnight reconciliation diffs and the team channel
workday: Design reviews, code reviews, and on-call tasks on rotation
evening: Replays the day's incidents in her notebook

relationships.name: Head of Risk
relationships.description: Routine sign-off relationship for any change that touches the settlement state machine.

other_facts: current_stack: Rust services on Kubernetes, Postgres, Kafka, GitHub Enterprise with self-hosted runners.
jobs_to_be_done: Delegate small, scoped units of work to an agent without losing the property that every merged change has an explicit human approver.
jobs_to_be_done: Get credible first drafts of finicky-but-bounded changes — a fee-table migration, a release note, a dependency bump — so review attention can go where it matters.
jobs_to_be_done: Keep the team's review queue moving without lowering what gets through.
pains: Almost-right edits that pass the existing tests but break invariants the tests do not yet cover.
pains: Flows that do not cleanly separate 'drafted' from 'ready to merge'.
pains: Tools where the model and prompt that produced a change are not recorded next to the diff.
adoption_yes_if: Every agent action lands as a draft pull request under a non-human bot identity, gated behind the same branch protections as the rest of the team's work.
adoption_yes_if: She can pin which agent and which model are used per workflow without forking config.
adoption_yes_if: She can run the workflow end to end against a single ticket on her laptop before enabling it in CI.
rejection_no_if: A default path lets agent-authored code merge itself.
rejection_no_if: One workflow's output can silently mutate state another workflow depends on.
anti_goals: She is not optimizing for the wall-clock time of a single agent run.
anti_goals: She is not interested in reducing human-in-the-loop touches as an end in itself.
recognition_cues: Phrases like 'what's the rollback', 'what model', 'show the diff first', 'draft, not merge'.
recognition_cues: Asks for the exact bot identity and branch-protection interaction before engaging with feature pitches.
recognition_cues: Treats 'auto-merge on green' as a smell rather than a feature.
